questions.text: The graph of a polynomial y = p(x) intersects the x-axis at exactly two distinct points and opens downwards. Which of the following is true for the polynomial p(x) = ax² + bx + c?
options: a > 0 and it has two distinct real zeroes
a < 0 and it has two distinct real zeroes
a = 0 and it is a linear polynomial
a < 0 and it has no real zeroes

questions.text: A student plots the graph of y = p(x). The graph does not touch or intersect the x-axis at all, but it intersects the y-axis at (0, -5). What can be concluded about the zeroes of p(x)?
options: The polynomial has one zero at x = -5.
The polynomial has no real zeroes.
The polynomial has infinite real zeroes.
The polynomial has two equal real zeroes.
It has two distinct real zeroes.
It has two coincident (equal) real zeroes.
It is not a quadratic polynomial.
questions.text: If the graph of a quadratic polynomial touches the x-axis at exactly one point, what does this signify algebraically about its zeroes?

options: It is a quadratic polynomial with degree 2.
It is a cubic polynomial because of the fraction.
It is not a polynomial at all.

questions.text:
**Assertion (A):** The highest exponent of the variable in the algebraic expression is called the degree of a polynomial.
**Reason (R):** The degree of $2x-5$ is $1$.

options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.text:
**Assertion (A):** Zeroes of $f(x)=x^2-4x-5$ are $5, -1$.
**Reason (R):** The polynomial whose zeroes are $2+\sqrt{3}$ and $2-\sqrt{3}$ is $x^2-4x+7$.

**Assertion (A):** If $(2-\sqrt{3})$ is one of the zero of the quadratic polynomial, then the other zero will be $(2+\sqrt{3})$.
**Reason (R):** Irrational zeroes (roots) always occur in pairs.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A

A is false but R is true

| Column I | Column II |
| :--- | :--- |
| (A) $p+q-\frac{2}{pq}$ | (i) $3$ |
| (B) $\frac{1}{p}+\frac{1}{q}-pq$ | (ii) $14$ |
| (C) $3p+3q+2pq$ | (iii) $2$ |
| (D) $5pq-2p-2q$ | (iv) $-3$ |
